PCF: Page Configuration File — Guidewire's XML-based UI framework used to define screens, widgets, and layouts in PolicyCenter, ClaimCenter, and BillingCenter.
Gosu: Guidewire's proprietary scripting language, based on Java syntax. Used to write business logic, rules, and customizations within Guidewire applications. Every Gosu customization is a potential upgrade liability.
PolicyCenter: Guidewire's Policy Administration System (PAS). Manages the full policy lifecycle including quotes, new business, endorsements, renewals, and cancellations.
ClaimCenter: Guidewire's Claims Management System. Handles the end-to-end claims lifecycle from FNOL through settlement and closure.
BillingCenter: Guidewire's Billing Management System. Manages premium billing, payment plans, disbursements, and agency billing.
InsuranceSuite: Guidewire's integrated suite of core insurance systems — PolicyCenter, ClaimCenter, and BillingCenter — deployed together on a shared platform.
GWCP: Guidewire Cloud Platform — Guidewire's SaaS offering that hosts InsuranceSuite in a managed cloud environment, replacing on-premises deployments.
FNOL: First Notice of Loss — the initial report made to an insurance company describing a loss or damage event. Triggers the claims process in ClaimCenter.
SmartCOMM: Smart Communications' document composition platform. Used in Guidewire implementations for generating policy documents, EOBs, and customer correspondence.
Exstream: OpenText's enterprise document composition platform used for generating insurance policy documents and communications. Functionally similar to SmartCOMM.
EIS: EIS Group's insurance core platform (PolicyCore, BillingCore, ClaimCore, CustomerCore) — a competing core system to Guidewire used by some P&C carriers.
Duck Creek: A P&C insurance platform provider offering Policy, Billing, and Claims systems. A key competitor to Guidewire in the core systems market.
Majesco: A cloud-native insurance platform provider, competing with Guidewire and Duck Creek in the P&C core systems space.
MuleSoft: Salesforce's integration platform (Anypoint Platform). Used to build APIs and integration flows connecting Guidewire to external systems like payment gateways, rating services, and portals.
SAFe: Scaled Agile Framework — an enterprise-scale Agile methodology used for coordinating large delivery teams across multiple workstreams with Program Increment (PI) Planning cadences.
PI Planning: Program Increment Planning — a SAFe ceremony where all teams align on a common mission and identify cross-team dependencies for the upcoming 10-week increment.
SCRUM: An Agile framework using short iterative sprints (typically 2 weeks) with daily standups, sprint planning, sprint review, and retrospectives.
RAG: Retrieval Augmented Generation — a GenAI pattern that grounds LLM responses in a specific document corpus. Used in claims and underwriting automation to process insurance documents intelligently.
LangChain: An open-source framework for building LLM-powered applications. Used to chain together prompts, retrievers, and tools to build RAG pipelines and AI agents.
SLA: Service Level Agreement — contractually defined performance commitments covering response times, resolution times, and uptime targets, typically for Application Managed Services (AMS).
AMS: Application Managed Services — post-go-live support and operations for Guidewire and related systems, including incident management, upgrades, monitoring, and enhancements.
SOW: Statement of Work — the contractual document defining project scope, deliverables, timeline, and commercial terms for a professional services engagement.
RACI: Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed — a matrix used in project management to clarify roles and ownership for tasks and decisions across teams.
RFC: Request for Change — a formal document initiating a change to project scope. Typically triggers a change order and commercial adjustment.
P&C: Property and Casualty — the segment of insurance covering damage to property (homeowners, commercial property) and liability for accidents (auto, workers comp, general liability).
WC: Workers Compensation — a line of P&C insurance providing wage replacement and medical benefits to employees injured on the job. CompSource Mutual is an example mono-line WC carrier.
LOB: Line of Business — a specific category of insurance product, such as personal auto, homeowners, commercial property, or workers compensation.
DOI: Department of Insurance — the state regulatory body overseeing insurance companies, products, and rates. Carriers must file rate changes and new products with the DOI for approval.
P&L: Profit and Loss — the financial measure of a business unit or engagement, calculated as revenue minus all associated costs. AVP-level leaders own P&L accountability.
CI/CD: Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment — automated pipelines that build, test, and deploy code changes frequently and reliably, reducing deployment time from hours to minutes.
DevOps: A set of practices combining software development and IT operations, emphasizing automation, CI/CD pipelines, and continuous delivery to shorten release cycles.
ETL: Extract, Transform, Load — the process of pulling data from source systems, transforming it to a target schema, and loading it into a destination like Snowflake or a data warehouse.
Terraform: HashiCorp's Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool. Used to provision and manage cloud resources (AWS, Azure, GCP) declaratively through configuration files.
Snowflake: A cloud-native data warehouse platform. Used in insurance analytics for loss ratio analysis, regulatory reporting, and self-service BI dashboards.
IaC: Infrastructure as Code — managing and provisioning cloud infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files (Terraform, CloudFormation) rather than manual processes.
API: Application Programming Interface — a defined contract for how two software systems communicate, typically over HTTP using REST or SOAP protocols.
AWS: Amazon Web Services — Amazon's cloud computing platform. Key services used in insurance delivery: EC2, S3, Lambda, Glue, Kinesis, and API Gateway.
Azure: Microsoft Azure — Microsoft's cloud computing platform. Provides Azure OpenAI, DevOps tooling, and infrastructure services used in Guidewire cloud integrations.
GCP: Google Cloud Platform — Google's suite of cloud computing services, including compute, storage, AI, and data analytics products.
Kubernetes: An open-source container orchestration platform for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.
Docker: A platform for packaging applications and their dependencies into portable containers that run consistently across development, staging, and production environments.
NPS: Net Promoter Score — a customer loyalty metric measured by asking how likely customers are to recommend a product or service, scored from -100 to +100.
PartnerConnect: Guidewire's partner program with tiers (Select, Advantage, Premier) for consulting and technology firms. Higher tiers reflect deeper certified expertise and transaction volume.
dbt: Data Build Tool — an open-source framework for transforming data in cloud warehouses using SQL. Used with Snowflake for insurance analytics and reporting.
Kafka: Apache Kafka — a distributed event streaming platform used for real-time data pipelines and event-driven architectures between Guidewire and downstream systems.
MoSCoW: A requirements prioritization framework: Must have (non-negotiable), Should have (important but not critical), Could have (nice-to-have), Won't have (deferred). Used in scope triage to protect go-live dates.
RFP: Request for Proposal — a formal document issued by a carrier soliciting competitive bids from vendors for a defined technology engagement.
QBR: Quarterly Business Review — a structured meeting between client and vendor to assess delivery performance, surface issues early, and align on the next quarter's roadmap.
UAT: User Acceptance Testing — a testing phase where the carrier's business users validate that the Guidewire system meets their requirements before production go-live.
SIT: System Integration Testing — testing phase that validates end-to-end data flow and functionality across Guidewire and all integrated downstream systems.
STAR: Situation-Task-Action-Result — a structured framework for answering behavioral interview questions with concrete, story-based examples.
SOX: Sarbanes-Oxley Act — US federal law mandating strict financial governance and IT controls. Relevant in regulated industries where audit trails and access controls are required.
T&M: Time and Materials — a contract model where the client pays for actual hours worked at agreed billing rates, used when scope is not fully defined upfront.
MVP: Minimum Viable Product — the smallest scoped release that delivers core business value, used to reduce initial project risk and get to go-live faster.
POC: Proof of Concept — a small-scale prototype built to validate the feasibility of a technology approach before committing to a full implementation.
POV: Point of View — a thought leadership document sharing an organization's perspective on an industry trend or challenge, used to establish credibility with clients.
Studio: Guidewire Studio — the Eclipse-based IDE and configuration tool used by developers to define product models, PCF screens, workflow states, and Gosu rules.
iFoundry: ValueMomentum's proprietary AI-powered platform and accelerator library for P&C insurance transformation, automation, and CELENT Model Insurer Award-winning solutions.
go-live: The production launch date when a new Guidewire system goes live and begins processing real insurance transactions for the carrier.
hypercare: An intensive post-go-live support period (typically 60–90 days) with elevated staffing and faster SLAs to stabilize the production environment.
scope creep: Uncontrolled expansion of project scope through informal requirement additions without corresponding changes to timeline, budget, or team capacity.
technical debt: Accumulated shortcuts, workarounds, and deferred improvements in a codebase that increase future maintenance cost and slow down delivery velocity.
post-mortem: A structured retrospective conducted after a project or incident to analyze root causes, identify what worked, and agree on process improvements.
on-prem: On-premises — software and infrastructure deployed and operated in the carrier's own data centers, as opposed to cloud-hosted (e.g., Guidewire Cloud Platform).
underwriting: The insurance process of evaluating an applicant's risk profile and determining whether to offer coverage, at what price, and under what terms.
loss ratio: A key insurance profitability metric: total claims paid divided by total premiums earned. A loss ratio above 100% means the carrier is paying more in claims than it collects.
canary deployment: A deployment strategy that rolls out new code to a small subset of users or servers first to validate stability before a full production release.
data migration: The process of extracting, transforming, and loading legacy policy, claims, or billing data into Guidewire during an implementation.
blended rate: The weighted average billing rate across a delivery team combining onshore (higher-rate) and offshore (lower-rate) resources.
utilization: The percentage of a team member's hours that are billed to a client engagement. Target is typically 80–85% in professional services.
bench: Unbilled employees between engagements — a direct cost to the firm. Proactively managing bench through pipeline alignment is a key AVP responsibility.
pipeline: The forward-looking portfolio of sales opportunities in various stages of pre-sales and proposal, representing future contracted revenue.
land and expand: A growth strategy of winning an initial engagement with a carrier (the 'land'), then systematically expanding into additional workstreams or systems (the 'expand').
Everest Group: A global research and advisory firm specializing in technology services. Known for the PEAK Matrix, which ranks service providers across industries including P&C insurance IT services.
PEAK Matrix: Everest Group's Provider Evaluation and Assessment Knowledge Matrix — a research framework ranking IT service providers as Leaders, Major Contenders, or Aspirants based on capability and market impact.
ISG: Information Services Group — an IT research and advisory firm that publishes the ISG Provider Lens reports ranking technology service providers globally.
CELENT: An Oliver Wyman research and advisory firm focused on financial services and insurance technology, known for the Model Insurer Awards recognizing digital innovation.
AMER: Americas — a geographic region designation used in Guidewire's PartnerConnect program to scope partner specializations to North and South America.
REST: Representational State Transfer — the dominant architectural style for web APIs, using standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to manage resources.
Azure OpenAI: Microsoft's enterprise deployment of OpenAI's GPT models on Azure cloud infrastructure, used for building GenAI applications in regulated environments.
EOB: Explanation of Benefits — a document sent to policyholders or claimants detailing what was covered, what was paid, and what the insured owes. Often generated via SmartCOMM or Exstream.
ISO: Insurance Services Office — a data analytics company providing insurance industry data, rating information, policy forms, and actuarial tools used in underwriting and pricing.
Verisk: A data analytics and risk assessment company providing insurance-specific data, rating models, and predictive analytics used by carriers in underwriting and claims.
integration layer: The middleware and API infrastructure connecting Guidewire to external systems — payment gateways, rating services, document composition, and third-party data providers.
downstream: Systems that consume or depend on data produced by Guidewire — such as billing processors, data warehouses, document systems, and regulatory reporting platforms.
greenfield: A brand-new implementation built from scratch with no legacy system constraints. In Guidewire context, a carrier adopting Guidewire for the first time without migrating from an existing core system.
brownfield: An implementation that replaces or modernizes an existing legacy system, requiring data migration and coexistence planning. More complex than a greenfield — the old system must keep running during transition.
LLM: Large Language Model — a deep learning model (e.g., GPT-4, Claude) trained on large text corpora, capable of generating, summarizing, and reasoning over natural language. Foundation of GenAI applications.
GenAI: Generative AI — AI systems that can generate text, images, code, or structured output from prompts. In insurance, used for claims intake automation, underwriting summarization, and document processing.
Agile: An iterative software delivery methodology that delivers working software in short cycles (sprints), adapts to changing requirements, and emphasizes collaboration between business and engineering teams.
sprint: A fixed time-boxed iteration (typically 2 weeks) in SCRUM where a team commits to delivering a specific set of user stories or features.
velocity: A SCRUM metric measuring how many story points a team completes per sprint. Used for forecasting delivery timelines and identifying productivity trends.
backlog: A prioritized list of all features, requirements, bugs, and tasks for a project. The product backlog is owned by the Product Owner; the sprint backlog is committed by the team for a given sprint.
burndown: A chart tracking remaining work (story points or tasks) over time within a sprint or release. A healthy burndown trends consistently toward zero by the sprint end date.
standup: A brief daily team sync (typically 15 minutes) where each member shares what they did yesterday, what they'll do today, and any blockers. Core SCRUM ceremony.
CIO: Chief Information Officer — the executive responsible for an organization's IT strategy, systems, and technology investments. Often the primary client sponsor in Guidewire implementation programs.
AVP: Associate Vice President — a senior leadership title in professional services and financial services firms, typically responsible for managing client relationships, delivery portfolios, and a team of managers.
SME: Subject Matter Expert — a person with deep expertise in a specific domain (e.g., Guidewire ClaimCenter, workers compensation) consulted for requirements, decisions, and quality assurance.
KPI: Key Performance Indicator — a quantifiable metric used to evaluate success against defined objectives (e.g., on-time delivery rate, client satisfaction score, utilization rate).
ROI: Return on Investment — a measure of the financial benefit of an investment relative to its cost. Used to justify technology programs to insurance carrier executives.
IP: Intellectual Property — proprietary assets such as software accelerators, reference architectures, frameworks, and methodologies developed by a firm. ValueMomentum's iFoundry is an example of delivery IP.
CoE: Center of Excellence — a centralized team with specialized expertise (e.g., Guidewire CoE, GenAI CoE) that sets standards, builds reusable assets, and supports project teams across an organization.
reinsurance: Insurance purchased by an insurance carrier from another insurer to reduce risk exposure. Allows carriers to underwrite larger policies by transferring a portion of risk to reinsurers.
endorsement: A policy change made mid-term that modifies coverage, adds or removes a driver/property, or adjusts premium. Processed in PolicyCenter as a policy change transaction.
renewal: The annual or periodic process of re-issuing an existing insurance policy for another term. Triggers underwriting review, rating, and updated policy documents.
cancellation: The termination of an insurance policy before its expiration date, initiated by either the carrier or the policyholder. Processed in PolicyCenter with pro-rata or short-rate premium calculations.
premium: The amount a policyholder pays for insurance coverage, calculated based on risk factors, coverage type, and rating rules. Collected and managed through BillingCenter.
deductible: The amount a policyholder must pay out-of-pocket before insurance coverage applies to a claim. A key coverage parameter configured in PolicyCenter's product model.
reserve: Funds set aside by an insurance carrier to pay future claims obligations. In ClaimCenter, claim reserves are set and updated throughout the claims lifecycle.
IBNR: Incurred But Not Reported — a reserve estimate for claims that have occurred but haven't yet been reported to the carrier. A key actuarial calculation for financial reporting.
combined ratio: A key insurance profitability metric: loss ratio + expense ratio. A combined ratio below 100% indicates underwriting profit; above 100% indicates underwriting loss.
submission: In underwriting, the package of information submitted by an agent or broker to a carrier requesting a commercial insurance quote — includes application, loss history, and financials.
lean: A management philosophy focused on eliminating waste, maximizing value delivery, and continuous improvement. Principles like value stream mapping are applied in delivery optimization.
value stream: The end-to-end sequence of activities required to deliver value to a customer — from initial request through production deployment. Used in lean delivery to identify and eliminate waste.
IP reuse: The practice of applying previously built accelerators, frameworks, and components across multiple client engagements to reduce effort and improve delivery consistency.
